Oil Prices Slide as Traders Prepare for Blockade to End

Crude oil prices are falling, with the sharpest declines in the spot market and near-dated futures contracts, even as global petroleum inventories deplete rapidly to the lowest for the time of year since 2008.

The entire forward pricing curve has flattened significantly since the end of April after steepening in the first two months of the war between the United States and Iran.

The speed and scale of the depletion would ordinarily have resulted in a much bigger rise in spot and near-dated futures contracts and a fiercer backwardation.

But traders have become increasingly convinced tanker traffic through the Strait will resume soon, either as a result of a diplomatic agreement or increased military protection of vessels by the United States ...

ANOTHER HAPPENING DAY IN THE MARKET

1) Markets sold off sharply on Wednesday, with the Dow dropping 953 points (-1.87%) to close at 49,918.78, the S&P 500 falling 1.62% to 7,267.65, and the Nasdaq shedding 1.98% to close at 25,169.50. The twin catalysts were a hot May CPI print and a fresh escalation in the Middle East, with President Trump threatening new strikes on Iran after Tehran shot down a U.S. Apache helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz.

2) May CPI came in at 4.2% year-over-year, the highest inflation reading in three years, with motor fuel prices up 41% compared to a year ago. Energy alone accounted for over 60% of the monthly CPI increase. Core CPI (ex-food and energy) rose 0.2% month-over-month and 2.9% year-over-year, which was broadly in line with estimates. Markets are now pricing a 25-bps rate hike before year-end, with Deutsche Bank noting that the Fed is increasingly focused on inflation after three straight above-consensus jobs reports.

3) $SMCI was the session's biggest large-cap collapse, plunging 27.98% to $29.27 on 186.3M shares (market cap ~$17.6B) after the AI server manufacturer announced plans to raise $7 billion through equity and equity-linked financing transactions to fund component purchases needed to satisfy roughly $39 billion in AI server orders. Investors punished the stock on dilution fears, with Seeking Alpha calling the move a forced sale driven by reputational risk and balance sheet concerns.

4) Oil surged as geopolitical tensions flared, with Brent crude closing above $93.10 per barrel, up 1.8% on the day, as President Trump threatened additional strikes on Iran and Iran signaled it could halt shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. WTI crude tracked higher as well, climbing nearly 2% in the session. The $USO ETF gained 2.28%, while airline stocks buckled under the fuel-cost implications.

5) Airlines were among the hardest hit sectors as oil spiked: $UAL fell 6.25% to $102.78, $DAL slid 5.79% to $76.47, and $AAL dropped 4.76% to $13.42 on 136.3M shares. Seeking Alpha noted the selloff was driven by fears of sharply rising jet fuel costs as Middle East tensions threatened sustained elevated oil prices, hitting carriers with minimal fuel hedging.

6) The semiconductor sector continued its painful rotation lower, with the chip sell-off accelerating after Broadcom's disappointing revenue forecast last week. $AVGO fell 5.12% to $372.10 on 37.4M shares (market cap ~$1.76T), $AMD dropped 4.86% to $452.40 on 27.2M shares, $MU slid 4.70% to $891.88 on 54.0M shares, and $NVDA lost 3.73% to $200.42 on 156.7M shares. Reuters flagged the broader narrative as "Sizzling AI now fizzling," with investors increasingly concerned about stretched AI valuations ahead of major IPOs including SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI.

7) $CBRL Cracker Barrel was a rare bright spot, surging 22.56% to $44.49 on 11.3M shares after reporting Q3 fiscal 2026 earnings that blew past estimates: EPS of $0.29 vs. analyst consensus of negative $0.48, and revenue of $797.4M vs. $776.7M expected. The company also raised its full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance to $120M-$125M and raised full-year revenue guidance, signaling the logo controversy that plagued the brand in 2025 is firmly in the rearview mirror.

8) $ORCL Oracle reported Q4 fiscal 2026 results after the close: EPS of $2.11 vs. $1.96 estimated (8% beat), and revenue of $19.18B vs. $19.1B expected, up 21% year-over-year. The company raised its adjusted EPS outlook for the year and reiterated its $90B revenue target for fiscal 2027. However, shares fell 7% in after-hours trading as investors focused on capital expenditures surging to $55.66B, a 162% increase, stoking concerns that AI data center buildout costs are ballooning faster than cloud revenue can support them.

9) Cantor Fitzgerald raised price targets across the semiconductor equipment complex in a sweeping note, lifting $KLAC to $2,500 from $2,000, $LRCX to $425 from $320, and $AMAT to $650 from $575, citing sustained robust investment in wafer fabrication equipment and continued AI-driven demand for advanced chip tooling. Seeking Alpha reported the note, with Cantor adjusting its CY26 WFE forecast upward. Despite the upgrades, KLAC slipped 0.17%, LRCX fell 1.64%, and AMAT edged down 0.44%, as the broader tech selloff offset the analyst tailwind.

10) Daiwa more than doubled its price target on $MU Micron Technology to $1,600 from $700, one of the largest single-day price target raises in the semiconductor space in recent memory. Daiwa cited the company's upcoming fiscal Q3 report on June 24, where consensus is modeling $33.5B in revenue with an 81% gross margin, reflecting what the firm sees as a structural shift in the memory market driven by AI-related HBM demand. MU closed at $891.88, implying roughly 79% upside to the new target.

11) $HOOD Robinhood rose 3.09% to $86.36 on 41.3M shares, bucking the market decline. Bloomberg reported that Cantor Fitzgerald raised its price target to $110 from $100, citing upside from prediction markets as Robinhood's Rothera joint venture captures the majority of exchange economics from its growing prediction markets volumes. Goldman Sachs also raised its target to $108 from $105. A Barron's report noted that a director bought roughly $35M of stock last week via a venture fund.

12) The top 10 most active options today by contracts traded were $TSLA with 3.67M contracts, $NVDA with 3.23M contracts, $AAPL with 1.74M contracts, $AMZN with 821K contracts, $SMCI with 696K contracts, $MSFT with 605K contracts, $META with 579K contracts, $ORCL with 569K contracts, $MU with 530K contracts, and $HOOD with 506K contracts. SMCI's activity ran 229% of its daily average, and HOOD ran 154% of average. The session's most-traded single contract was the NVDA Jun 10 $200 put with 335K contracts, a bearish bet on Nvidia closing exactly at or below the psychologically significant $200 level.

13) $CAVA surged 6.92% to $81.56 (market cap ~$9.5B) after UBS upgraded the Mediterranean fast-casual chain to Buy from Neutral, calling for a breakout in unit economics and comparable store sales as the brand expands nationally. UBS did not issue a specific price target in the publicly available note, but the upgrade bucked a session where Consumer Cyclical as a whole fell 1.93%.

14) Defensive stocks were the day's standouts: Consumer Defensive gained 0.62% and Financial Services rose 0.38%, the only two S&P sectors to close in the green. $KO Coca-Cola rose 2.77% to $83.59, hitting an all-time high during the session, as Morgan Stanley reiterated Overweight and identified it as a top pick, citing sustained outperformance driven by its Fairlife milk brand expansion. $TJX jumped 1.69% to $167.66, also setting a fresh record high amid a flight to defensive retail.

June 10, 2026 US Consumer Price Index CPI Report

MarketMaestro

21 hours ago
The market is locked onto Consumer Price Index, CPI, data. This data set is seen as the most critical macroeconomic crossroads that will determine market direction on the eve of the first Federal Open Market Committee, FOMC, meeting under new Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh, amid the energy shocks created by escalating geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East, new tariff walls in supply chains, and unprecedented structural cost increases created by artificial intelligence investments.

General Consensus and Expectation Ranges

According to analyst estimates collected by FactSet, headline CPI is expected to rise 0.5% month over month in May and climb from the previous month’s 3.8% level to 4.2% year over year. Core CPI, which excludes volatile items such as food and energy and is the main focus of monetary policy, is expected to rise 0.3% month over month and reach 2.9% year over year.

Goldman Sachs: The institution, which has largely abandoned rate cut expectations because of labor market resilience, expects Core Personal Consumption Expenditures, PCE, inflation to gradually fall to 2.1% by December.

JPMorgan Chase: The institution’s Chief US Economist Michael Feroli draws attention to the fragile impact of the May CPI data on market psychology. According to Feroli, an upside inflation surprise above expectations would completely shatter the inflation is transitory narrative in markets and pull forward the timing of the first rate hike. JPMorgan’s models show that in such an inflationary scenario, the probability of the Fed hiking rates by September 2026 would exceed 50%, and that a second hike would start to be priced by December. I have seen this institution be wrong many times.

Morgan Stanley: Matt Hornbach says the May CPI data to be released will be spicier, and that the pressure created by high energy and AI infrastructure costs will keep inflation alive. On the other hand, Chief Global Economist Seth Carpenter expects global growth to be 3.2% in 2026 and inflationary pressures to soften as oil prices decline toward $90 by year end.

Deutsche Bank: The bank offers one of the most aggressive headline inflation estimates on Wall Street, 4.29%, based on its projection of a sharp 6.8% monthly increase in gasoline prices specifically for CPI.

Citi and Barclays: Citi analysts confirm that headline inflation will jump to 4.2%, while saying the real key question is whether there will be a month over month slowdown in core CPI.

Real time market data, platforms such as Zillow, and independent rent indexes show that new rent increases across the US have largely stopped, and that real rent costs are easing as housing supply increases and landlords activate aggressive incentives such as free months to find tenants. However, the survey based OER calculation method used by the BLS follows market realities with a 6 to 12 month lag. As Jim Bianco from Bianco Research also noted, a statistical anomaly caused by the government shutdown threat last September continues to artificially inflate shelter figures. This methodological lag will continue to cause shelter inflation to remain high on paper in the May CPI report, despite cooling in the real economy.

For the May CPI to be released in June, the T-1 signal in Truflation systems produced warnings before the official print, indicating that live market pricing in key components points to upward pressure and that the official print is likely to surprise. However, Truflation Chief Data Officer Oliver Rust says: High figures from the BLS are entirely due to instant jumps in energy and methodological statistical errors in shelter; despite the strong surface appearance in the labor market, the underlying core economy is actually softening rapidly.

Kevin Warsh will chair the first FOMC meeting of his career on June 16-17.

Warsh is expected to completely remove the rate cut friendly bias toward easing from the official statement and shift to a neutral stance policy position. The new Fed Chair is expected to adopt a strategy of keeping rates high for longer by citing stubborn CPI data, in order not to repeat the past inflation is transitory mistake.

Market Scenarios After the Data

If headline inflation approaches Deutsche Bank’s extreme estimates and pushes toward 4.3%, and especially if core goods, because of tariffs, and shelter, because of lagged OER effects, surprise to the upside, the initial market reaction could be extremely destructive.

If the data comes exactly in line with the FactSet median and Bank of America estimates, markets will quickly calm down after the data volatility and shift focus to the FOMC meeting one week later and Kevin Warsh’s communication.

If headline data falls below 4.0% and core inflation records an increase lower than 0.2%, this could create excitement in markets. But this probability currently looks difficult.

The Cleveland Fed’s dynamic model expects headline inflation at 4.05%, below consensus, and projects a more stable path for core inflation at 0.23% month over month and 2.83% year over year. On the other hand, Truflation’s real time data fell to a low level of 1.83%. They see OER and shelter items in BLS data as structural noise.

🦇Batman Rational Forecast for May 2026 CPI

• Headline CPI, monthly: 0.38% to 0.42% range. Direct reflections of the geopolitical energy shock will be effective, but consensus seems overly pessimistic.

• Headline CPI, yearly: 4.0% to 4.1% range. Slightly below the market consensus of 4.2%, closer to the Cleveland Fed’s 4.05% expectation.

• Core CPI, monthly: 0.20% to 0.25% range.

• Core CPI, yearly: flat around 2.8%.

Ultimately, although the data that will hit screens on the morning of June 10 will look uncomfortable at the headline level because of high energy costs and lagged shelter effects, when looking into core details, it may carry signs of structural cooling. The new Federal Reserve leadership under Kevin Warsh will have to take this geopolitical noise in headline figures into account while building its communication strategy, but in the background, it will be aware that restrictive monetary policy is creating a disinflationary gravitational pull in real consumer demand.

In this scenario, if the market chooses tomorrow to strip out the energy and shelter noise in the headline and buy the cooling in the core, we may see a controlled upward move. However, a print at or above the consensus boundary could send hedging costs in the market flying ahead of Kevin Warsh. Because since this is the new Fed Chair’s first meeting, nobody wants to take risk in this uncertainty.

A TON OF THINGS HAPPENED IN THE STOCK MARKET TODAY.

Here's a full recap:

1. Iran announced a full shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz following U.S. strikes on southern Iran, sharply escalating tensions in one of the world’s most critical oil-shipping routes. Iranian state media also said Iran’s military attacked the U.S. Fifth Fleet in Bahrain in response to the strikes. President Trump is now holding a White House Situation Room meeting to discuss potential additional strikes, per Axios. Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, today said: "We will be hitting Iran hard tonight, they have had their time to negotiate and these attacks are not a restart to the war but to set the terms for the deal."

2. Oracle $ORCL reported FY26 capex of $55.7B, above its prior $50B plan, after Q4 capex reached $15.9B, and guided FY27 capex to a massive $90B–$95B, with around $70B expected to be cash capex as it ramps AI data center spending. The company also plans to raise roughly $40B through debt and equity, including its $20B ATM program, to fund the buildout. In Q4’26, Oracle reported revenue of $19.2B, slightly above estimates and up 21% YoY, adjusted EPS of $2.11, beating estimates and up 24% YoY, operating income of $8.6B, ahead of expectations, and cloud revenue of $9.9B, up 47% YoY.

3. Palantir $PLTR CEO Alex Karp made several notable comments on CNBC about Anthropic, AI labs, Maven, and why Palantir is winning with enterprises. On Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, Karp said “most of the things they talk about in public are running on Palantir,” adding that Amodei has gone from being “way behind” to “ahead” and that he genuinely believes what he is saying. Karp also said the public has only seen older versions of Maven, arguing Americans would be “positively surprised” by the advances in U.S. defense technology. More broadly, he criticized AI labs for operating with what he called a “hyper-religion of hyper-optimism,” saying they believe they can solve all problems — including human nature and even the problems they create themselves. His bigger point was that the models alone do not create value; the real value comes from implementation, deployment, and turning AI into actual workflows, which is why Palantir is winning with enterprises.

4. U.S. inflation came in line with expectations in May, but remained elevated. Headline CPI rose 4.2% YoY, matching estimates, while core CPI increased 2.9% YoY, also in line with expectations. It marks the hottest inflation reading since 2023, but because both headline and core CPI matched what the market was looking for, the report was more about confirming inflation remains sticky than delivering a major surprise.

5. Citadel Securities is making a simple point in its new “Tokenomics” note: AI may be revolutionary, but it still has to obey economics. As token bills rise, companies are being forced to think about compute costs, power, cooling, memory bandwidth, and inference budgets. Citadel says the market is already shifting toward cheaper “good enough” models, creating a split where only the biggest players can afford frontier AI at scale. 

6. OpenAI is reportedly considering major price cuts for its AI services as it looks to win more enterprise customers from Anthropic, per WSJ. The company is weighing significant reductions to token pricing, a move that could pressure profit margins across the AI industry given the massive compute costs required to run advanced models. The push comes as Anthropic’s Claude Code has gained traction with software engineers and helped the company rapidly grow revenue, forcing OpenAI to compete more aggressively on price. A broader AI pricing war could benefit customers by lowering costs, but it may also make the economics tougher for both OpenAI and Anthropic as enterprises look for cheaper ways to use AI at scale.

We are witnessing a textbook example of a massive, temporary liquidity drain.

The 3 macro realities defining the tape today:

Look at tomorrow's Nasdaq debut of SpaceX (Ticker: SPCX). Institutional order books show a staggering $250 billion in capital chasing a massive, yet limited, $75 billion primary share allocation. Desks are aggressively raising cash, liquidating liquid "winners" across the board just to fund their target allocations for the largest IPO in stock market history.
2/ Kinetic Escalation & The Energy FloorWhile the equity market deals with this massive capital rotation, geopolitical variables are adding structural friction. Following renewed cross-border military strikes in the Middle East, Brent crude has stabilized right back near the $95/barrel threshold. Energy isn't just an abstract headline; it is the absolute floor keeping global headline inflation sticky and unpredictable.

3/ The ECB and the Transatlantic Yield TrapAcross the Atlantic, the European Central Bank is facing its own policy crossroads today. With U.S. inflation accelerating to 4.2% and energy supply chains fractured, fixed-income investors are demanding a structural risk premium on sovereign bonds. The fantasy of a smooth, coordinated global central bank easing cycle is officially over.
The Bottom Line:Today’s market friction is the result of a perfect storm: An unprecedented distortion of global dollar liquidity caused by a historic $1.75T mega-IPO running parallel to raw, persistent inflation.

Once the SpaceX pricing clears tonight and allocations are finalized, the massive wave of oversubscribed, unused cash sitting on the sidelines will have to find its way back into the market. Until then, tactical patience and cash optionality remain the superior play.

ULTRA-DETAILED FACT-FILLED NEWS REPORT
Thursday, June 11, 2026

1. TOP STORY — U.S.–IRAN FIGHTING THREATENS FRAGILE CEASEFIRE

The most serious global story today is the renewed exchange of attacks between the United States and Iran. The U.S. and Iran traded fire for a second day, putting a fragile ceasefire under major strain. The fighting reportedly escalated after a U.S. Army Apache helicopter went down near the Strait of Hormuz, with President Donald Trump blaming Iran and ordering retaliatory strikes on Iranian military infrastructure.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps responded with missile attacks aimed at U.S.-linked positions in the Gulf region, including bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan. Iran also threatened shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most important oil transit routes. U.S. officials said commercial vessels were still moving through the strait despite Iran’s threats.

Why it matters: this is not just another regional clash. If the Strait of Hormuz becomes unsafe, energy prices can rise quickly, shipping insurance costs can surge, and global markets can shake. Oil prices jumped earlier in the day before easing as traders weighed whether Iran’s threats would actually stop shipping.

The G7 summit scheduled for June 15–17 in Evian-les-Bains, France, is now expected to be dominated by Iran, Ukraine, and the question of whether Western leaders can manage multiple crises at once.

2. GLOBAL MARKETS — WAR FEARS AND AI STOCK WEAKNESS HIT CONFIDENCE

Global markets are under pressure from two forces at the same time: Middle East conflict risk and anxiety over the technology sector. Asian shares moved lower after another sell-off in AI-related stocks dragged on Wall Street. Investors are watching oil, interest rates, corporate AI spending, and geopolitical risk together.

Oil rose as traders reacted to the U.S.–Iran escalation, though prices pulled back from earlier highs. Brent crude was reported around the low-to-mid $90s per barrel range, while U.S. crude also remained elevated. The concern is not only the current price but the possibility of a sudden supply shock if shipping through the Gulf is disrupted.

Technology stocks remain sensitive because investors are questioning whether massive AI spending will translate into profits fast enough. Oracle’s aggressive spending plans raised debt concerns, while broader AI-sector selling has made markets more cautious.

Main takeaway: investors are not panicking blindly, but they are becoming more defensive. War risk, oil prices, AI valuations, and interest-rate expectations are now feeding into one another.

3. RUSSIA–UKRAINE WAR — UKRAINE STRIKES DEEPER INTO RUSSIAN ENERGY AND LOGISTICS

Ukraine is intensifying long-range attacks on Russian energy, logistics, and military supply networks. Russian-controlled Sevastopol in Crimea suspended fuel distribution after supply routes were disrupted. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy praised recent strikes on Russian energy and logistics targets, framing them as a way to weaken Russia’s ability to continue the war.

Reports say Ukrainian drones and missiles have hit refineries, oil infrastructure, bridges, occupied ports, and supply routes tied to Russian military operations. One Russian refinery in Samara reportedly halted operations after a drone strike. Ukraine has also highlighted its expanding drone-war capabilities, with June 11 designated as a day to honor its unmanned systems forces.

At the same time, Ukraine faces a serious energy problem. A think tank warned that Russian attacks on Ukraine’s power infrastructure could cause summer shortages and consumer outages. Under hotter conditions or further damage, shortages could become severe.

Why it matters: the war is moving deeper into infrastructure warfare. Ukraine is trying to degrade Russia’s military supply chain, while Russia continues targeting Ukraine’s civilian energy system. Both strategies increase pressure far beyond the front line.

4. MIDDLE EAST BEYOND IRAN — GAZA, LEBANON, AND REGIONAL ESCALATION REMAIN DANGEROUS

The Middle East remains highly unstable beyond the direct U.S.–Iran exchange. Gaza continues to face severe humanitarian strain, and the broader Israel–Iran–Hezbollah regional confrontation remains a major danger point. Recent reporting also shows concerns over lawlessness and internal violence in Gaza, including reports that Hamas members executed or maimed Palestinians accused of collaboration.

Lebanon remains under intense pressure from the Israel–Hezbollah conflict, with displacement and civilian suffering adding to the danger of a wider war. The key risk is that one crisis can ignite another: U.S.–Iran fighting, Israeli operations, Hezbollah actions, and Gaza instability all overlap.

Main takeaway: the region is not facing one isolated conflict. It is facing a chain of connected conflicts, where escalation in one place can quickly spread to another.

5. U.S. WEATHER — SEVERE STORMS AND EARLY SUMMER HEAT RISKS

Severe weather is a major U.S. concern today, especially across parts of the Midwest and Great Lakes. Michigan faces a significant severe storm threat, with damaging winds, large hail, heavy rain, and possible tornadoes. Some forecasts warned that wind gusts could become especially dangerous if organized storm lines develop.

NOAA’s Weather Prediction Center also continues to monitor heavy rainfall and storm systems across parts of the country. The broader summer pattern includes heat risk building later in June, especially across Florida, the Southeast, parts of the Mid-Atlantic, and areas of the Southern Plains.

NOAA’s 2026 Atlantic hurricane outlook calls for a below-normal season overall, with a 55% chance of below-normal activity, a 35% chance of near-normal activity, and a 10% chance of above-normal activity. That does not mean “no hurricanes.” Even a quiet season can produce a destructive landfall.

Practical takeaway: people in storm-prone areas should watch local alerts, keep phones charged, know their shelter location, and not rely on the overall hurricane-season forecast as a personal safety guarantee.

6. HEALTH — EBOLA OUTBREAK IN CONGO REMAINS SERIOUS

The Democratic Republic of Congo’s Ebola outbreak remains a serious global health story. Reuters reported that confirmed cases have climbed sharply, with hundreds of confirmed infections and dozens of deaths. Earlier WHO updates showed suspected cases dropping after many were ruled out, but the outbreak remains dangerous because of the Bundibugyo strain and supply shortages affecting medical workers.

Reports from Congo describe health workers facing shortages of protective supplies, including boots and masks. U.S. officials have also announced additional funding for Ebola response, while specialized U.S. treatment centers say they are prepared to handle Ebola cases if necessary.

Why it matters: Ebola outbreaks are controlled by fast detection, isolation, contact tracing, protective equipment, public trust, and medical support. Shortages among frontline workers increase risk, not only to patients but to the people trying to stop the outbreak.

7. TECHNOLOGY AND AI — GOVERNMENTS, COMPANIES, AND ARTISTS PUSH FOR SAFETY AND CONTROL

Artificial intelligence remains one of the biggest technology stories of 2026. The White House said it wants to accelerate AI development and use for national security while saying it should not be used for unlawful surveillance. The administration also wants leading AI developers to submit powerful models for cybersecurity testing before release.

At the same time, Anthropic is urging the U.S. not to block state-level AI laws unless there is a strong federal standard. The concern is that AI is advancing too quickly for weak regulation, but fragmented laws could also create confusion.

In the music world, Deezer launched a free AI music detector for users of major streaming platforms. This reflects a growing fight over AI-generated songs, artist rights, platform transparency, and whether listeners should know when music is machine-made.

Main takeaway: AI is no longer just a tech-company issue. It is now a national security issue, a state-law issue, a copyright issue, a music-industry issue, and a trust issue.

8. RELIGION AND CULTURE — POPE LEO XIV DRAWS MASSIVE CROWD IN SPAIN

Pope Leo XIV drew a reported 1.2 million people to Mass in Madrid during a major visit to Spain. He challenged Europe to acknowledge Christianity’s deep contribution to its identity, including art, culture, schools, hospitals, and moral formation.

The event included Spain’s religious and cultural traditions, including processions, music, and public devotion. A large youth turnout was also reported, suggesting renewed interest in public religious expression among younger people.

However, the visit also took place under the shadow of the Catholic Church’s sexual abuse crisis in Spain, with survivors continuing to raise concerns about transparency and accountability.

Main takeaway: Europe’s struggle over faith, secularism, memory, and moral identity remains very alive. The crowd size shows that public Christianity still has deep roots, even in increasingly secular societies.

9. CONSTRUCTIVE DEVELOPMENTS — CANCER SURVIVAL AND TARGETED MEDICINE

One hopeful health development is the continued progress in targeted cancer therapies. Reuters reported that more people are living longer with cancer as targeted drugs improve survival and disease management for certain patients.

This does not mean cancer has been “solved.” It means treatment is becoming more precise for some cancers, allowing certain patients to live longer, manage disease better, and continue daily life while receiving therapy.

Why it matters: modern medicine is moving from one-size-fits-all treatment toward targeted approaches based on the biology of a person’s disease. That can mean better outcomes, fewer wasted treatments, and more hope for patients who once had fewer options.

10. BIG PICTURE — WHAT TO WATCH NEXT

The biggest watchpoints over the next several days are:

1. Whether U.S.–Iran fighting escalates or returns to negotiations.
2. Whether shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains open.
3. Whether oil prices stay elevated or spike higher.
4. Whether the June 15–17 G7 summit produces serious crisis-management steps.
5. Whether Ukraine’s long-range strike campaign further disrupts Russian fuel and logistics.
6. Whether Russia intensifies attacks on Ukraine’s power grid during summer heat.
7. Whether severe U.S. storms produce major damage in the Midwest and Great Lakes.
8. Whether the Ebola outbreak in Congo accelerates or begins to come under better control.
9. Whether AI regulation moves toward national standards or remains fragmented.
10. Whether public trust in news, government, medicine, and technology continues to weaken or begins to stabilize.

BOTTOM LINE

Today’s news is dominated by escalation, pressure, and uncertainty. The U.S.–Iran confrontation is the most immediate global danger because it touches war, oil, shipping, diplomacy, and markets at once. Ukraine is expanding deep-strike warfare while facing energy shortages at home. AI is becoming more powerful and more contested. Public health officials are watching Ebola closely. Severe weather is threatening parts of the U.S.

Yet there are also signs of resilience: medicine is advancing, churches and public faith gatherings still draw large crowds, and governments are being forced to confront risks that can no longer be ignored.

This is a day to watch carefully, pray soberly, and refuse shallow headlines.

Oil rises more than $1 as escalation in US-Iran strikes unnerve traders

Oil prices jumped on Thursday as Tehran declared the critical energy chokepoint, the Strait of Hormuz, closed ‌after the U.S. launched additional strikes against Iran and as President Donald Trump vowed even more attacks if no peace deal is secured.

Brent futures rose $1.48, or 1.59%, to $94.58 a barrel as of 0243 GMT, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude climbed $1.71, or 1.90%, to $91.74. U.S. crude ​futures gained more than $3 earlier in the session.

Iran's top joint military command announced the closure of the ​Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, including oil tankers and commercial ships, saying any vessel attempting ⁠passage will be shot at.

"It once again suggests a deal is still some way off and that energy ​flows from the Persian Gulf will remain heavily constrained," said ING analysts in a note to clients.

The renewed ​escalation in fighting prompted oil prices to rally in early morning trading, they said.

On Wednesday, the U.S. military said on X that commercial ships continue to transit in and out of the strait.

It also said no U.S. warships have been struck in the strait, after ​Iran's state media reported U.S. ships near the waterway were targeted by missiles and drones.

U.S. forces began launching ​additional strikes against multiple targets in Iran at 5:15 p.m. EDT (21:15 GMT), the latest in an escalating exchange of attacks that threaten ‌to ⁠reignite a full-scale war, which was paused in early April when the two sides agreed to a fragile ceasefire.

Trump told Fox News reporter Trey Yingst on Wednesday evening that the strikes would stop shortly but that he would "bomb the shit out of them" if Iran's leaders did not sign an agreement with the U.S. immediately.

Iran's months-long ​blockade of the strait, which ​normally carries a fifth ⁠of global oil and gas shipments, has kept oil prices elevated.

Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories fell by 7.2 million barrels to 426.5 million barrels in the week ended ​June 5, the EIA said on Wednesday, compared with analysts' expectations in a Reuters ​poll for a ⁠4 million-barrel draw.

U.S. crude inventories, including those from strategic reserves, have fallen by 79 million barrels since the Iran war began on February 28, as the top global producer moved to plug supply gaps after the strait was effectively ⁠shut.

Underscoring the ​squeeze, OPEC output in May slid to its lowest level in ​over two decades, a Reuters survey showed, as a U.S. naval blockade curbed Iran's exports and Tehran's effective closure of the strategic waterway slashed ​shipments from other Gulf producers. - REUTERS

June 10, 2026 Economic Calendar Recap: CPI In Line with Slight Core Cooling Today’s key release was the US May CPI report, which came in largely as expected: Headline CPI rose 0.5% MoM (matching consensus)
Core CPI (ex-food & energy) came in softer at +0.2% MoM (better than +0.3% expected)
Year-over-year, headline CPI accelerated to 4.2% from 3.8%, while core held at 2.9%

The data shows persistent inflation pressures (especially in shelter and energy), even as monthly core readings moderate slightly. This keeps the Federal Reserve in a data-dependent stance with limited urgency for near-term rate cuts. Other Notable Data: Canada BoC held its policy rate at 2.25% as widely expected, with a steady press conference.
EIA Crude Oil Inventories showed a larger-than-expected draw of -7.23M barrels, supporting oil prices.
Russia and UK inflation figures also released with limited market impact.

Market Implications:
The combination of sticky headline CPI and strong oil prices adds to a cautious backdrop. Tech and growth stocks faced rotation pressure today, while energy names benefited. Markets remain focused on sector rotation, geopolitical risks, and upcoming earnings. Bottom Line: No major surprises in CPI, but the re-acceleration in headline inflation prevents a dovish shift. Traders should stay nimble with key support/resistance levels in major indices.
